title: Undefined Behavior and How to Avoid It
content:
Undefined behavior (UB) means the compiler makes no guarantees about what happens. In safe Rust, UB is impossible (that is the whole point of the borrow checker). In unsafe Rust, you can trigger UB by violating invariants the compiler assumes.

UB does not necessarily crash immediately -- it can cause subtle corruption, work 'fine' in debug mode but fail in release, or break seemingly unrelated code. This makes it extremely dangerous.

The main sources of UB in Rust: dangling/null pointer dereference, data races, invalid values (e.g., bool that is not 0 or 1), breaking aliasing rules (multiple &mut to same memory), and uninitialized memory reads.
keyPoints: UB means the compiler makes zero guarantees about behavior
UB can cause silent corruption, not just crashes
Main UB sources: dangling pointers, data races, invalid values, aliasing violations
Use Miri (cargo +nightly miri test) to detect UB in tests
NonNull provides a non-null pointer guarantee
When in doubt, avoid unsafe and use safe standard library abstractions
